steps.text: Consult this policy in determining whether to be a cosponsor of the conference.

preconditions.text: Other Federal agencies are cosponsoring a conference with nonfederal organizations.
The request for CDC sponsorship is received on or after October 1, 2008.

constraints.text: Public health decisions must be based on sound science and the public good.
The benefits to society must be a higher goal than benefits to either partner in the collaboration.
The agency must be a diligent steward of public trust and funds.
The agency and its employees should conduct business according to the ethical standards that govern the agency.
Scientific meetings and conferences should be unbiased forums for exchange of scientific information and consider the implications for public health.
Scientific meetings and conferences should provide venues for open scientific discourse.
Organizers of scientific meetings and conferences should provide a transparent process for selecting presentations based primarily on scientific merit.

steps.text: Obtain approval from the director of the CC/CO/NC(s), or his/her designee, for requests for CDC sponsorship of conferences that include funding.
Process the request through financial support procedures as described in Section 4H (1) or (2).
Obtain approval from the director of the CC/CO/NC(s), or his/her designee, for requests for CDC sponsorship of conferences that do not include funding.

preconditions.text: The conference supports CDC's mission and furthers CDC's mission of promoting health and quality of life by preventing and controlling disease, injury, and disability.
Scientific meetings meet the Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences outlined in Section 3 of this policy (when applicable).
Sponsorship is consistent with CDC's policy on Guidance for Collaboration with the Private Sector and the HHS Office of the General Counsel (OGC), Ethics Division Co-Sponsorship Guidance.

constraints.text: Any conference sponsored by CDC that is held in domestic facilities must be fully accessible to the public as required by the Americans with Disabilities Act Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG).

steps.text: Clear all promotional or publicity materials for the event with CDC to ensure compliance with restrictions on using CDC's name.
Approve or disapprove requests for technical assistance, participation in, or attending a conference by CDC staff members according to procedures determined by the employee's CC/CO/NC.
Ensure that CDC-sponsored conferences/meetings are managed in a transparent, objective, and unbiased manner.
Adhere to CDC Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences.

Use existing conference grant or contract mechanisms or develop written guidelines and processes that are in accordance with existing procurement and ethics laws, rules and policies, when funding conferences and meetings.

preconditions.text: If the sponsor is a non-federal sponsor

constraints.text: The conference organizer(s) may use CDC's name only in factual publicity for the conference and should understand that CDC involvement in the conference does not necessarily indicate support for, or endorsement of, the organizer's general policies, activities, or products.
CDC sponsored conferences will be guided by sound scientific and programmatic oversight.
Certain processes may vary between different CCs/COs/NCs, scientific subject areas, and conference types, and will be tailored as necessary to the individual needs of each conference/meeting.

steps.text: Make contingency award allowing usage of only 10% of the total amount to be awarded.
Release the remainder of funds.

preconditions.text: The final full agenda is approved by CDC.

constraints.text: CDC funds will not be expended for nonapproved portions of meetings.
All scientific conferences receiving CDC funds must meet CDC’s Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences and follow relevant policies and procedures.
CDC reserves the right to approve or reject the content of the full agenda, press events, promotional materials, speaker selection, and site selection.

steps.text: Submit a formal request in writing to the CCs/COs/NCs and/or the OD, CDC, providing the organization's mission, purpose, capacity, proposed content/format, intended audience, and other cosponsors.
Review the submitted request (CC/CO/NC Director or designee, or OD in coordination with CC/CO/NC).
Provide a written response to the conference organizer(s) indicating disapproval of the request.
Provide a written response to the conference organizer(s) indicating tentative approval, containing the specified CDC participation language.
Review the full final conference agenda and other conference information.
Provide a written response to the conference organizer(s) indicating final approval or disapproval of sponsorship.
Complete a co-sponsorship agreement with the non-federal co-sponsor.

preconditions.text: Sponsorship of a conference is requested and does not involve expenditure of CDC funds
The CC/CO/NC or the OD, CDC, does not wish to sponsor the conference
The CC/CO/NC is interested in sponsoring the conference
Final approval of sponsorship is provided

constraints.text: No verbal disapproval of sponsorship may be provided.
No verbal approval of sponsorship may be provided.

steps.text: Obtain written certification of ADA compliance from facility management, or complete the Accessible Meetings Checklist and keep it on file.

Verify that the written certification or documentation addresses minimum disability access issues, including parking, entrances, doors, wheelchair space, water/toilet facilities, fixed seating, sales counters, and auxiliary aids.
Assure that the proposed meeting facilities comply with ADAAG prior to approving any request or issuing any obligation documents.

preconditions.text: ADA compliance for the meeting facility has already been assured, documentation is maintained, and the facility has not undergone any renovation.

constraints.text: All conferences sponsored by CDC shall be held in facilities that are fully accessible to the public as required by American with Disabilities Act Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG).
A public accommodation must provide auxiliary aids and services when they are necessary to ensure effective communication with individuals with hearing, vision, or speech impairments.

steps.text: Maintain a copy of all written correspondence regarding sponsored conferences on file in the Office of the Director of the CC/CO/NC having programmatic responsibility.
Ensure records include documentation required as per Section 4H.
Account fully and accurately for each party's programmatic and financial responsibilities and activities in agreements and records.
Describe separately in agreements and records any discrete portion of an event that will be exclusively sponsored by CDC or exclusively sponsored by a non-Federal entity.
Make agreements and records concerning the amounts, sources, and uses of funds available to the public upon request.
Assign a specific person responsibility for maintaining these records and for submitting a quarterly line listing of all conferences sponsored or disapproved to the OD, CDC.
Maintain records of these listings at the OD and make them available to the CC/CO/NCs upon request.

constraints.text: CDC shall not co-sponsor an event with an entity that will not make information concerning funding publicly available.
The definition of sponsorship/co-sponsorship should be consistent with that provided in the HHS Co-Sponsorship Guidance.

Co-sponsorship excludes prospective co-sponsors that would provide only funding for an event, as well as prospective co-sponsors that do not have a demonstrable substantive interest in the subject matter of the event.

steps.text: Assess how the potential collaboration fits within CDC's overall mission and priorities and the private partner's mission and priorities.
Address independence and objectivity of scientific judgment in the potential collaboration and identify mechanisms to ensure objectivity and independence.
Assess the effect of the private partner's products or services, if applicable, on health and whether they are compatible with CDC's mission.

Assess the behavior of the private partner in conducting business and determine whether the partner's behavior is consistent with CDC's mission and the principles guiding private sector collaboration.
Assess the public impact of the partner's broad public mission and image, seeking multiple collaborators representing a broad spectrum of opinions and interests if the issue is highly controversial.
Avoid participating in indirect collaborations unless a direct relationship with the partners would be acceptable.
Inform private partners that the existence of the collaborative relationship will be made a matter of public record and work out early how this will occur.
Maintain a process of openness and fairness in developing collaborations with partners on similar projects or projects with similar intent.
Establish a formal review and advisory process within each coordinating center (CC), coordinating office (CO), or national center (NC) for examining potential collaborations.

constraints.text: No direct endorsement of a product or service is permitted by the agency.

steps.text: Receive orientation and guidance from the CDC Ethics Officer concerning the agency's principles, criteria, and recommendations for collaborating with the private sector.

preconditions.text: Employee will be developing collaborations with the private sector.
